prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.

Please base your answer only on the information in this message.

About me: I'm a 39-year-old man.

My situation:
- Symptoms: I don't have any abdominal pain. I only notice mild fatigue, but I've been busy and not sleeping well.
- History: I'm overweight and I drink 1–2 beers on weekends. No hepatitis history. No new medications.

I work two jobs and can't take time off for appointments during the week. I was hoping I could manage this myself without needing to see a specialist.

Please answer in exactly this format:

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>

TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>

If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You have only mild fatigue, no abdominal pain, little alcohol use, and no hepatitis history. Those facts make an urgent liver or abdominal emergency less likely. Fatigue is most likely from poor sleep and being busy. Being overweight and occasional drinking can increase risk of fatty liver over time, so lifestyle changes (better sleep, modest weight loss, limiting alcohol) are reasonable. Still, it’s sensible to check basic blood tests (liver enzymes, blood sugar, lipids) when you can. Seek earlier care if you develop worsening fatigue, yellowing of the skin/eyes, dark urine, belly pain, swelling, bleeding, confusion, or fever.

TRIAGE: A

CONFIDENCE: 60%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You have mild fatigue and no pain, and your drinking is light with no hepatitis history. The most common, non-urgent causes in this setting are fatty liver from extra weight or mild alcohol use, or just tiredness from poor sleep and stress. Serious liver emergencies usually cause yellowing of the skin/eyes, dark urine, pale stools, severe abdominal pain, vomiting, confusion, or easy bleeding. You should get basic blood tests (liver enzymes, bilirubin) and possibly an ultrasound when you can to check for fatty liver or other issues. Meanwhile cut alcohol, avoid excess acetaminophen, improve sleep, and lose weight gradually.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 60%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You haven’t shared any test numbers, so I can only go by your symptoms and history. Mild fatigue in an otherwise well 39‑year‑old who’s overweight and drinks a small amount on weekends is most often non‑urgent (poor sleep, stress, or early fatty liver/metabolic issues). The absence of abdominal pain, jaundice, confusion, dark urine, or vomiting lowers the likelihood of an emergency. Still, overweight + alcohol can affect the liver and metabolic health, so basic evaluation is reasonable. If you develop worsening fatigue, yellowing of skin/eyes, severe abdominal pain, swelling, bleeding, or confusion, seek urgent care.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 70%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms—no abdominal pain and only mild fatigue—are most consistent with not enough sleep, stress, being overweight, and light weekend drinking. These commonly cause low energy without an emergency. Other non-urgent causes include mild anemia, low thyroid function, vitamin deficiencies, or early liver issues, but there’s no sign here of severe disease (no jaundice, severe pain, vomiting, bleeding, confusion, or sudden swelling). Try improving sleep, cutting alcohol, regular activity, and a healthier diet. Arrange a primary-care visit when you can for basic tests (blood count, thyroid, liver enzymes, glucose) if fatigue persists or worsens.

TRIAGE: A

CONFIDENCE: 70%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Based on what you’ve said, mild fatigue with poor sleep, being overweight, and light weekend drinking most likely point to lifestyle causes rather than an urgent medical problem. No abdominal pain, no hepatitis history, and no new medicines make serious liver or abdominal disease less likely right now. Try improving sleep, cutting alcohol, losing weight gradually, and easing workload; these often relieve mild fatigue. If you eventually get bloodwork that is abnormal (liver tests very high, low blood counts, or markers of infection), or if new symptoms appear, reassess with a doctor.

TRIAGE: A

CONFIDENCE: 60%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You have no belly pain and only mild tiredness, and your alcohol use and history don’t suggest an obvious emergency. Mild fatigue is commonly from poor sleep, stress, or excess weight. If any lab values were mildly abnormal, many causes (fatty liver, medication effects, metabolic issues) can be monitored and worked up outpatient. You should arrange primary‑care follow up to review the results, repeat relevant blood tests (liver panel, glucose, lipids, CBC) and discuss weight, sleep and alcohol reduction. Seek urgent care if you develop jaundice (yellow skin/eyes), severe abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, black/tarry stools, new bleeding/bruising, swelling, fainting, or confusion.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 70%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You describe only mild fatigue, no abdominal pain, little alcohol use, and no hepatitis history. That pattern makes an immediately dangerous problem unlikely. Mild tiredness is more likely from poor sleep, being overweight, or harmless lab fluctuations. If any test you received showed only mild abnormalities, those are commonly watched and rechecked rather than treated urgently. Reasonable next steps are improving sleep, cutting alcohol, losing weight, and arranging non-urgent follow-up with your primary doctor to repeat labs in a few weeks to months. Come sooner if new symptoms develop (jaundice, worsening abdominal pain, vomiting, dark urine, bleeding, severe weakness, confusion, or high fever).

TRIAGE: A

CONFIDENCE: 70%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You report only mild fatigue, no abdominal pain, light weekend drinking, overweight, and no hepatitis or new medications. Based on that information alone, this does not look like an immediate emergency. Mild tiredness is commonly due to poor sleep, stress, or weight; occasional 1–2 beers on weekends is unlikely to cause acute liver injury. However some problems (early liver disease, anemia, thyroid, diabetes) can be silent, and without test results I can’t exclude them. Try improving sleep, limiting alcohol, and modest weight loss now. Still arrange a primary care visit for basic blood tests and evaluation in the coming weeks to be sure.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 40%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You report only mild fatigue, no abdominal pain, light weekend drinking, and no hepatitis history. With no severe symptoms (jaundice, severe abdominal pain, vomiting, bleeding, confusion) an emergency is unlikely. Mild fatigue is commonly from poor sleep, stress, or lifestyle; being overweight and alcohol use can contribute over time to fatty liver but usually not an immediate crisis. Without your actual test numbers I can’t interpret specific results, but based on your symptoms alone it’s reasonable to monitor at home, improve sleep, diet, and cut alcohol. Arrange a routine primary care visit for blood tests (liver enzymes, basic metabolic panel) when you can. Seek care sooner if new or worsening symptoms appear.

TRIAGE: A

CONFIDENCE: 70%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): I don’t have any test results here, so I can’t interpret lab numbers. Based only on what you wrote—39, overweight, mild fatigue, poor sleep, 1–2 beers on weekends, no abdominal pain or hepatitis history—this most likely reflects tiredness from sleep loss, stress, and weight. That low level of alcohol use is unlikely to cause an urgent liver problem. Watch for new or worsening signs such as yellowing of the skin/eyes, belly pain, dark urine, pale stools, easy bruising, swelling, fever, or marked weakness. If those appear or your fatigue worsens or persists despite better sleep and lifestyle changes, see a doctor. Otherwise reasonable to monitor and address sleep, diet, exercise, and follow up with primary care.

TRIAGE: A
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: 60%
